channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: The real costs of prohibition | #GFN26 Panel
item.description: The real costs of prohibition are paid by consumers, communities and public health systems. When safer nicotine products are banned or heavily restricted, demand does not disappear — it shifts towards illicit markets, unsafe supply chains and continued cigarette use. These policies can increase stigma, reduce trust and make harm reduction harder to access. Evidence-based regulation should reduce risk in the real world, not create new harms in pursuit of perfect control. (Published on July 02, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: Ireland moves closer to banning fruit and sweet vape flavours
item.description: Ireland’s move towards banning fruit and sweet vape flavours risks weakening one of the features that helps adult smokers switch away from cigarettes. Flavours can make vaping more acceptable than smoking and help people distance themselves from the taste of combustible tobacco. Protecting young people is essential, but broad flavour bans may reduce the appeal of lower-risk alternatives for adults trying to quit. Better regulation should target youth access without removing tools that support smoking cessation. (Published on July 02, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)

item.title: On Nicotine, FDA Embraces Science and Delivers a Public Health Victory
item.description: The FDA’s authorisation of modified risk claims for ZYN nicotine pouches marks a public health win for evidence-based risk communication. It recognises a simple but often ignored fact: smoke-free nicotine products do not carry the same risks as combustible cigarettes. For adult smokers, clear information about lower-risk alternatives can support better decisions and more realistic routes away from smoking. Public health should follow the science, not blur all nicotine products into the same category. (Published on July 02, 2026byrealclearscience.com)

item.title: The nanny state is sanitising Britain to death
item.description: Britain’s generational smoking ban reflects a wider shift towards treating adults as problems to be managed rather than people capable of informed choice. Reducing smoking matters, but prohibition is a blunt tool that risks ignoring autonomy, pleasure and real-world behaviour. Harm reduction offers a better path: give people accurate information, preserve safer alternatives and reduce risk without sanitising life into submission. Public health should help adults make better choices, not remove choices altogether. (Published on July 02, 2026byspiked-online.com)

item.title: Switching from a failed vape and tobacco policy to a successful one
item.description: Australia’s vape and tobacco policy shows the cost of making safer products harder to access than cigarettes. Legal vapes are restricted through pharmacies, while combustible tobacco remains widely available, leaving smokers with fewer practical routes away from the most harmful option. A successful policy would regulate nicotine by risk, protect young people and keep lower-risk alternatives in legal, accountable channels. Harm reduction should replace a system that is failing both smokers and public health. (Published on July 02, 2026byjohnmenadue.com)
